title: How Honeybees Communicate
passage:
Honeybees have one of the most sophisticated systems of communication known among insects. When a forager bee discovers a rich source of nectar or pollen, she returns to the hive and performs a series of movements known as the waggle dance. During this dance, the bee moves in a figure-eight pattern, running straight through the middle of the loop while rapidly shaking her abdomen from side to side. The direction of this straight run, measured relative to the vertical, encodes the direction of the food source relative to the sun. The duration of the waggle portion communicates the distance: a longer waggle indicates that the food lies farther away.

Remarkably, other bees crowd around the dancer in the darkness of the hive, following her movements closely and even touching her with their antennae. Because the interior of the hive is dark, the audience cannot see the dance in the usual sense; instead, they perceive the vibrations and the airflow the dancer produces. After decoding this information, the recruited bees fly out and locate the food, often with impressive accuracy over distances of several kilometers.

The biologist Karl von Frisch first decoded the waggle dance in the mid-twentieth century, an achievement that earned him a Nobel Prize. His conclusion that a simple insect could convey abstract spatial information was initially met with skepticism, since many scientists doubted that such symbolic communication existed outside of humans. Later experiments, however, confirmed that bees genuinely use the dance to guide one another, though they also rely on scent cues to pinpoint the exact location once they arrive nearby.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Honeybees rely mainly on scent rather than movement to find food.
Honeybees use a dance to communicate the location of food sources.
Karl von Frisch was the first scientist to study insect behavior.
The interior of a beehive is too dark for bees to see one another.
questions.explanation: The passage centers on the waggle dance and how it conveys the direction and distance of food. The other choices describe supporting details or are contradicted by the text.

questions.q: According to the passage, what does the duration of the waggle run indicate?
options: How rich the food source is
The direction of the food relative to the sun
How far away the food source is
The number of bees needed to collect the food
questions.explanation: The passage states that a longer waggle indicates that the food lies farther away, so duration encodes distance. Direction is encoded by the angle of the run, not its duration.

questions.q: How do bees inside the hive perceive the dance if they cannot see it?
options: They smell the nectar carried by the dancer.
They sense the vibrations and airflow the dancer produces.
They follow a trail of pollen left on the honeycomb.
They listen for a high-pitched buzzing sound.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that because the hive is dark, the audience perceives the vibrations and the airflow the dancer produces rather than seeing the dance.

questions.q: Why was von Frisch's conclusion initially met with skepticism?
options: Many scientists doubted that symbolic communication existed outside of humans.
His experiments could not be repeated by other researchers.
Bees were thought to be unable to fly long distances.
The Nobel committee questioned the accuracy of his data.
questions.explanation: The passage says skepticism arose because many scientists doubted that such symbolic communication existed outside of humans.

title: The Life Cycle of Stars
passage:
Stars are not permanent fixtures of the sky; they are born, they age, and eventually they die. A star begins its life inside a giant cloud of gas and dust called a nebula. Over time, gravity causes regions of the cloud to collapse inward. As the material contracts, it heats up, and when the central temperature becomes high enough, nuclear fusion ignites. Fusion converts hydrogen into helium and releases enormous amounts of energy, which is what makes the star shine.

For most of its life, a star exists in a stable phase called the main sequence, during which the outward pressure from fusion balances the inward pull of gravity. Our own Sun is currently a middle-aged main-sequence star. How long a star remains on the main sequence depends chiefly on its mass. Counterintuitively, more massive stars burn through their fuel far more quickly than smaller ones and therefore have much shorter lives, sometimes lasting only a few million years rather than billions.

When a star exhausts the hydrogen in its core, its structure changes dramatically. The core contracts while the outer layers expand and cool, turning the star into a red giant. What happens next depends again on mass. A star like the Sun will shed its outer layers and leave behind a dense, slowly cooling remnant called a white dwarf. Much more massive stars end their lives in a catastrophic explosion known as a supernova, which can briefly outshine an entire galaxy and scatter heavy elements into space. These scattered elements later become the raw material for new stars and planets.

questions.q: What causes a star to begin forming within a nebula?
options: A nearby supernova pushes the gas together.
Gravity causes regions of the cloud to collapse inward.
Nuclear fusion begins spontaneously in the cold gas.
The cloud cools until it turns into solid dust.
questions.explanation: The passage states that gravity causes regions of the cloud to collapse inward, which begins the star-forming process. Fusion only ignites later, after the material has heated up.

questions.q: According to the passage, why do more massive stars have shorter lives?
options: They are located farther from other stars.
They contain less hydrogen to begin with.
They burn through their fuel far more quickly.
They never reach the main sequence stage.
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly says more massive stars burn through their fuel far more quickly and therefore have much shorter lives.

questions.q: What does the passage say happens to a star like the Sun after the red giant stage?
options: It explodes as a supernova.
It collapses directly into a nebula.
It sheds its outer layers and leaves a white dwarf.
It returns to the main sequence.
questions.explanation: The passage states that a star like the Sun will shed its outer layers and leave behind a white dwarf, while only much more massive stars become supernovae.

questions.q: Why are supernovae described as important beyond the death of a single star?
options: They scatter heavy elements that become material for new stars and planets.
They pull nearby stars into the main sequence.
They create nebulae that block starlight permanently.
They cause neighboring stars to collapse into white dwarfs.
questions.explanation: The passage notes that supernovae scatter heavy elements into space, and these later become the raw material for new stars and planets.

title: The Printing Press and the Spread of Ideas
passage:
Before the middle of the fifteenth century, books in Europe were copied by hand, usually by monks working slowly in monasteries. Producing a single copy of a lengthy text could take months, and as a result books were rare and expensive. Only the wealthy, the church, and a few institutions could afford large collections. This scarcity limited literacy and slowed the exchange of new ideas.

The situation changed dramatically after Johannes Gutenberg introduced a printing press with movable metal type around 1450. Movable type allowed individual letters to be arranged, inked, and pressed onto paper, then rearranged to print a different page. Once a page was set, hundreds of identical copies could be produced quickly. The cost of books fell sharply, and their numbers multiplied. Within a few decades, printing presses had appeared in cities across Europe.

The consequences reached far beyond the book trade. As printed materials became affordable, literacy spread among ordinary people, and information could travel faster and more widely than ever before. Religious reformers, scientists, and political thinkers used the press to circulate their arguments to large audiences, sometimes in defiance of established authorities. Historians often point to the printing press as a key factor in the Protestant Reformation and the Scientific Revolution, because it enabled controversial ideas to spread before they could be suppressed. In this way, a change in technology helped reshape the intellectual and social landscape of an entire continent.

questions.q: Why were books rare and expensive before the mid-fifteenth century?
options: Paper had not yet been invented in Europe.
They were copied slowly by hand.
Governments strictly limited who could own them.
Few people knew how to read them.
questions.explanation: The passage states that books were copied by hand and that producing a single copy could take months, which made them rare and expensive.

questions.q: What key feature of Gutenberg's press is emphasized in the passage?
options: It printed images as well as text.
It used water power to run automatically.
Its movable type could be rearranged for different pages.
It required no ink to make copies.
questions.explanation: The passage highlights movable metal type, which allowed letters to be arranged, pressed, and then rearranged to print a different page.

questions.q: According to the passage, what was one social effect of cheaper printed materials?
options: Literacy spread among ordinary people.
Monasteries grew wealthier from copying books.
Fewer people wanted to read new ideas.
Governments gained tighter control over information.
questions.explanation: The passage says that as printed materials became affordable, literacy spread among ordinary people and information traveled faster and wider.

questions.q: Why do historians connect the printing press to movements like the Reformation?
options: It allowed the church to censor opposing views quickly.
It enabled controversial ideas to spread before they could be suppressed.
It reduced the number of scientists in Europe.
It made handwritten books more valuable.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that the press enabled controversial ideas to spread widely before they could be suppressed, which is why it is linked to the Reformation and Scientific Revolution.

title: Supply, Demand, and Price
passage:
In a market economy, the prices of most goods are not set by any single authority. Instead, they emerge from the interaction of two forces: supply and demand. Demand refers to how much of a good buyers are willing and able to purchase at various prices. In general, when the price of a good rises, buyers tend to purchase less of it, and when the price falls, they purchase more. Supply refers to how much of a good producers are willing to offer for sale at various prices. Producers usually offer more of a good when its price is high, because higher prices make production more profitable.

The price at which the quantity buyers want to purchase exactly equals the quantity producers want to sell is called the equilibrium price. At this price, the market clears: there is neither a surplus of unsold goods nor a shortage of buyers left unsatisfied. If the price happens to be set above equilibrium, producers will offer more than buyers wish to purchase, creating a surplus that pushes prices back down. If the price is below equilibrium, eager buyers will compete for scarce goods, driving prices up.

These adjustments help explain why prices change over time. Suppose a poor harvest reduces the supply of a crop. With less available, the good becomes scarcer, and its price tends to rise until the quantity demanded falls to match the smaller supply. Conversely, if a new technology allows producers to make a good more cheaply, supply increases and prices tend to fall. Understanding these basic relationships allows economists to predict, at least roughly, how markets will respond to changing conditions.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How governments set the prices of goods
How supply and demand determine prices in a market
Why poor harvests always cause economic crises
How new technologies are invented
questions.explanation: The passage explains how the interaction of supply and demand determines prices, making that its central idea.

questions.q: According to the passage, what typically happens to demand when a price rises?
options: Buyers purchase more of the good.
Buyers purchase less of the good.
Producers offer less of the good.
The equilibrium price disappears.
questions.explanation: The passage states that when the price of a good rises, buyers tend to purchase less of it.

questions.q: What is the equilibrium price?
options: The highest price producers can legally charge
The price at which quantity demanded equals quantity supplied
The average of all prices over a year
The price set by the largest producer in a market
questions.explanation: The passage defines the equilibrium price as the price at which the quantity buyers want to purchase exactly equals the quantity producers want to sell.

questions.q: What does the passage predict will happen if a poor harvest reduces supply?
options: Prices tend to rise until demand matches the smaller supply.
Prices stay the same because demand is fixed.
Producers create a large surplus of the crop.
The equilibrium price falls sharply.
questions.explanation: The passage says that when supply falls, the good becomes scarcer and its price tends to rise until quantity demanded falls to match the smaller supply.

title: The Invention of Perspective in Painting
passage:
For much of the Middle Ages, European painters did not attempt to depict space in a realistic way. Figures were often arranged flatly on the surface, with important people drawn larger than others regardless of where they stood. Distant objects were not systematically made smaller, and there was little sense of a coherent three-dimensional world behind the picture's surface. Paintings were meant to convey spiritual meaning rather than to imitate the appearance of the visible world.

During the early fifteenth century in Italy, artists and architects developed a technique known as linear perspective that transformed the way space was represented. Linear perspective is based on the observation that parallel lines appear to converge as they recede toward a single vanishing point on the horizon, and that objects appear smaller the farther away they are. By following precise geometric rules, a painter could create the convincing illusion of depth on a flat panel or wall, so that a viewer seemed to look through the surface into a real space beyond.

The architect Filippo Brunelleschi is usually credited with demonstrating the principles of linear perspective, and the painter Masaccio was among the first to apply them in major works. The technique spread rapidly among Renaissance artists, who prized it as a mark of skill and sophistication. Perspective did more than improve realism; it reflected a broader cultural interest in mathematics, observation, and the rational ordering of the world. In this sense, a development in painting was closely tied to the wider intellectual currents of the Renaissance.

questions.q: How did medieval painters typically treat the size of figures?
options: They drew all figures exactly the same size.
They drew important people larger regardless of position.
They made distant figures systematically smaller.
They avoided including human figures entirely.
questions.explanation: The passage states that in medieval painting important people were drawn larger than others regardless of where they stood.

questions.q: What observation is linear perspective based on?
options: Colors appear brighter in the foreground of a scene.
Parallel lines appear to converge toward a vanishing point.
Shadows always fall toward the viewer.
Objects change color as they move closer.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that linear perspective is based on the observation that parallel lines appear to converge toward a single vanishing point and that distant objects appear smaller.

questions.q: Who is credited with demonstrating the principles of linear perspective?
options: Masaccio
Filippo Brunelleschi
An anonymous medieval monk
A group of Renaissance mathematicians
questions.explanation: The passage states that Filippo Brunelleschi is usually credited with demonstrating the principles of linear perspective, while Masaccio was among the first to apply them.

questions.q: According to the passage, what broader cultural interest did perspective reflect?
options: A renewed focus on spiritual meaning over realism
An interest in mathematics, observation, and rational order
A rejection of geometry in favor of intuition
A desire to return to medieval artistic styles
questions.explanation: The passage says perspective reflected a broader cultural interest in mathematics, observation, and the rational ordering of the world.

title: Why Coral Reefs Are Turning White
passage:
Coral reefs are among the most diverse ecosystems on Earth, supporting thousands of species of fish, invertebrates, and other marine life. Although a coral may look like a rock or a plant, it is actually a colony of tiny animals called polyps. Most reef-building corals share their tissues with microscopic algae known as zooxanthellae. Through photosynthesis, these algae produce sugars that provide the coral with much of its energy, and in return the coral offers the algae a sheltered place to live. This partnership also gives corals their vivid colors.

When ocean conditions become stressful, however, this relationship can break down. If the water grows too warm for even a few weeks, corals expel the algae living in their tissues. Without the colorful algae, the coral's white skeleton shows through, a phenomenon called coral bleaching. A bleached coral is not immediately dead, but it is weakened and deprived of its main source of food. If stressful conditions persist, the coral may starve and die; if conditions improve quickly enough, the algae can return and the coral may recover.

Rising ocean temperatures linked to climate change have made bleaching events more frequent and severe in recent decades. Large stretches of reef that once teemed with life have turned pale and, in some cases, died. Because reefs shelter coastlines from storms and support fisheries that millions of people depend on, their decline has consequences that extend well beyond the underwater world. Scientists are studying whether some corals can adapt to warmer water, but many warn that reducing greenhouse gas emissions is essential to protecting reefs over the long term.

questions.q: What is a coral, according to the passage?
options: A type of underwater plant
A colony of tiny animals called polyps
A kind of algae that produces sugar
A rock formation shaped by ocean currents
questions.explanation: The passage states that although a coral may look like a rock or plant, it is actually a colony of tiny animals called polyps.

questions.q: What causes coral bleaching?
options: Corals expel their algae when water becomes too warm.
Fish eat the colorful algae off the coral.
Storms scrape the color from the coral skeleton.
Corals stop producing their own pigment as they age.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that when water grows too warm, corals expel the algae in their tissues, and the white skeleton shows through, which is bleaching.

questions.q: According to the passage, is a bleached coral necessarily dead?
options: Yes, bleaching kills the coral instantly.
No, it is weakened but may recover if conditions improve.
Yes, because the algae can never return.
No, because bleaching does not affect the coral's food supply.
questions.explanation: The passage says a bleached coral is not immediately dead but weakened, and if conditions improve quickly the algae can return and the coral may recover.

questions.q: Why does reef decline matter beyond the underwater world?
options: Reefs are the main source of oxygen on Earth.
Reefs shelter coastlines and support fisheries people depend on.
Reefs prevent ocean temperatures from rising.
Reefs are the only home of zooxanthellae algae.
questions.explanation: The passage notes that reefs shelter coastlines from storms and support fisheries that millions of people depend on, so their decline has wider consequences.

title: The Bystander Effect
passage:
In social psychology, the bystander effect describes a surprising pattern: the more people who witness an emergency, the less likely any single person is to offer help. One might expect that a larger crowd would mean more potential helpers and therefore a faster response. Research, however, has repeatedly found the opposite. When many bystanders are present, each individual is less likely to intervene, and help may be delayed or never come at all.

Psychologists have proposed several explanations for this behavior. One is the diffusion of responsibility: when many people are present, each person feels less personally responsible for acting, assuming that someone else will step in. Another factor is social influence. People often look to those around them to decide how to behave in an ambiguous situation. If no one else appears alarmed, an individual may conclude that the situation is not actually an emergency, even when it is. A third factor is the fear of embarrassment, since people worry about overreacting or looking foolish in front of others.

These explanations were developed after researchers conducted controlled experiments in which participants faced staged emergencies, sometimes alone and sometimes in groups. Consistently, participants who believed they were alone helped more quickly and more often than those who thought others were present. Understanding the bystander effect has practical value: safety experts now advise that a person needing help should single out one specific individual and make a direct request, rather than appealing to a crowd in general. Naming one person cuts through the diffusion of responsibility and makes it far more likely that help will arrive.

questions.q: What does the bystander effect describe?
options: People help faster when more witnesses are present.
People are less likely to help when more witnesses are present.
People always help strangers in emergencies.
People help only when they are part of a large group.
questions.explanation: The passage defines the bystander effect as the pattern in which the more people who witness an emergency, the less likely any single person is to help.

questions.q: What is meant by 'diffusion of responsibility'?
options: People feel more responsible when others are watching.
Each person feels less responsible, assuming someone else will act.
Responsibility is shared equally and help comes quickly.
Only trained professionals feel responsible in emergencies.
questions.explanation: The passage explains diffusion of responsibility as each person feeling less personally responsible when many are present, assuming someone else will step in.

questions.q: How can social influence discourage helping in an ambiguous situation?
options: People imitate others who rush to help.
People conclude it is not an emergency if no one else seems alarmed.
People become more alarmed than the situation warrants.
People leave the scene to find authorities.
questions.explanation: The passage states that if no one else appears alarmed, an individual may conclude the situation is not an emergency, even when it is.

questions.q: What practical advice follows from research on the bystander effect?
options: Wait for a large crowd to gather before asking for help.
Single out one specific person and make a direct request.
Avoid asking for help in public places.
Appeal to the crowd in general as loudly as possible.
questions.explanation: The passage advises that a person needing help should single out one specific individual and make a direct request, which cuts through diffusion of responsibility.

title: How Fireflies Make Light
passage: Fireflies are among the most efficient light producers known. The glow they emit on summer evenings comes from a chemical reaction called bioluminescence, which takes place in specialized cells in the insect's abdomen. Within these cells, a compound called luciferin reacts with oxygen in the presence of an enzyme, luciferase. Unlike an incandescent bulb, which wastes most of its energy as heat, this reaction is remarkably cool: nearly all of the released energy becomes visible light rather than warmth. This efficiency matters, because a firefly cannot afford to overheat its own tissues. The purpose of the flashing is chiefly communication. Males of each species produce a distinctive pattern of pulses as they fly, and females perched on vegetation answer with their own timed flashes, allowing the two to find each other in the dark. Because each species follows a unique rhythm, the signals also help insects avoid mating with the wrong kind. Some predatory females, however, have learned to imitate the flash pattern of other species, luring unsuspecting males close and then devouring them. This deceptive mimicry shows that a signaling system meant to bring individuals together can also be exploited, turning an honest courtship display into a fatal trap.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How fireflies produce light and use it to communicate
Why summer evenings attract large numbers of insects
How incandescent bulbs could be made more efficient
The anatomical differences between male and female fireflies
questions.explanation: The passage first explains the chemical reaction that makes the light and then describes how the flashing is used to communicate, so both ideas together are the main topic.

questions.q: According to the passage, what is the role of luciferase in the light-producing reaction?
options: It stores oxygen within the insect's abdomen
It is an enzyme that allows luciferin to react with oxygen
It generates the heat that the reaction gives off
It sets the flashing rhythm unique to each species
questions.explanation: The text states that luciferin reacts with oxygen 'in the presence of an enzyme, luciferase.'

questions.q: It can be inferred from the passage that the flashing rhythm of a firefly species
options: is identical across all firefly species
is produced only by the females
changes completely from one night to the next
normally helps insects recognize members of their own species
questions.explanation: Because 'each species follows a unique rhythm' that helps insects 'avoid mating with the wrong kind,' the rhythm normally lets them identify their own kind.

questions.q: The word 'distinctive' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: faint
repeated
characteristic
confusing
questions.explanation: A 'distinctive pattern of pulses' is one that is characteristic of, and identifies, a particular species.

questions.q: Why does the author mention the incandescent bulb?
options: To suggest that fireflies may one day replace electric lights
To contrast a wasteful light source with the firefly's efficient one
To explain why fireflies are in danger of overheating
To describe how luciferin is manufactured in a laboratory
questions.explanation: The bulb, which 'wastes most of its energy as heat,' is contrasted with the firefly's reaction, in which 'nearly all of the released energy becomes visible light.'

title: The Life of Sunspots
passage: Sunspots are dark blemishes that appear from time to time on the visible surface of the Sun. They look dark only by comparison: a sunspot is still searingly hot, but it is cooler than the surrounding gas by more than a thousand degrees, and so it emits less light and stands out as a shadowy patch. These spots form where the Sun's magnetic field becomes intensely concentrated. The magnetic forces there are strong enough to slow the churning motion that normally carries heat up from the interior, so the surface at that point cools and darkens. Sunspots are not permanent; an individual spot may last from a few days to a couple of months before fading. Their numbers rise and fall in a rhythm that repeats roughly every eleven years, a pattern known as the solar cycle. At the peak of the cycle, dozens of spots may be visible at once; at its low point, the surface can remain nearly blank for weeks. Astronomers track these changes closely, because periods of high sunspot activity are often accompanied by violent eruptions that hurl charged particles toward Earth, disrupting radio signals, satellites, and power grids. The humble dark spot is thus a window into the Sun's restless magnetic behavior.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Sunspots are the coldest objects in the solar system
The surface of the Sun is completely covered in dark spots
Sunspots are cool, magnetically produced features that vary over a regular cycle
Radio signals on Earth control the number of sunspots
questions.explanation: The passage explains that sunspots are cooler regions caused by concentrated magnetic fields whose numbers follow the roughly eleven-year solar cycle.

questions.q: According to the passage, why does a sunspot look dark?
options: It is cooler than the gas around it and gives off less light
It contains no gas at all
It is much farther from Earth than the rest of the surface
It is blocked from view by charged particles
questions.explanation: The text says a sunspot 'is cooler than the surrounding gas... and so it emits less light and stands out as a shadowy patch.'

questions.q: It can be inferred that disruptions to satellites are most likely when
options: the Sun's surface is nearly blank
the solar cycle is near its peak
an individual sunspot lasts only a few days
the magnetic field disappears entirely
questions.explanation: Disruptions come from eruptions that accompany 'high sunspot activity,' and spot numbers are highest at the peak of the cycle.

questions.q: The word 'concentrated' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: weakened
hidden
cooled
gathered densely together
questions.explanation: For the magnetic field to become 'intensely concentrated' means its force is gathered densely into one area.

questions.q: According to the passage, how long can an individual sunspot last?
options: From a few days to a couple of months
Exactly eleven years
Several centuries
Only a few seconds
questions.explanation: The text states that 'an individual spot may last from a few days to a couple of months before fading.'

title: The Value of Salt Marshes
passage: Salt marshes are coastal wetlands that flood and drain with the daily tides. At first glance they may seem like wasteland, flat expanses of mud and coarse grass with little obvious value. In fact, they rank among the most productive ecosystems on Earth. The dense grasses that dominate them, chiefly cordgrass, trap sediment carried in by the tides, gradually building up the soil and raising the marsh surface. This same vegetation absorbs the energy of incoming waves, so a healthy marsh acts as a natural buffer that shields the land behind it from storm surges and erosion. Beneath the surface, the waterlogged, oxygen-poor mud slows decomposition, allowing the marsh to store large amounts of carbon that would otherwise enter the atmosphere. The marsh is also a nursery: many commercially important fish and shellfish spend their early lives among the protective stems before moving out to open water. Yet these habitats are increasingly threatened. Coastal development, pollution, and the steady rise of sea level all place pressure on marshes, and where they are hemmed in by seawalls they cannot migrate inland as the water rises. Protecting them, scientists argue, is far cheaper than building the artificial defenses that would be needed to replace their services.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: Why cordgrass is difficult to remove from farmland
The ecological value of salt marshes and the threats they face
How ocean tides are generated along the coast
The history of seawall construction
questions.explanation: The passage catalogs the benefits salt marshes provide and then describes the pressures that threaten them.

questions.q: According to the passage, how does marsh vegetation help protect the land behind it?
options: By releasing extra oxygen into the mud
By speeding up the decomposition of dead plants
By raising the temperature of the seawater
By absorbing the energy of incoming waves
questions.explanation: The grasses 'absorb the energy of incoming waves,' so the marsh 'shields the land behind it from storm surges and erosion.'

questions.q: It can be inferred from the passage that a marsh hemmed in by a seawall is at greater risk because
options: it traps far too much sediment
it stores far too much carbon
it cannot move inland as the sea level rises
it produces too many fish and shellfish
questions.explanation: The text notes that where marshes are 'hemmed in by seawalls they cannot migrate inland as the water rises,' leaving them squeezed as sea level climbs.

questions.q: The word 'buffer' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: protective barrier
food supply
narrow channel
warning sign
questions.explanation: A 'natural buffer that shields the land' functions as a protective barrier against waves and surges.

questions.q: Why does the author state that protecting marshes is 'far cheaper than building the artificial defenses'?
options: To argue that marshes should be replaced by seawalls
To explain how the tides drain the marsh each day
To show that marshes actually have little economic value
To emphasize the practical benefit of preserving the natural services marshes provide
questions.explanation: The comparison stresses that keeping the marsh's free 'services' is cheaper than paying to build structures that would replace them.

title: The Spacing Effect in Learning
passage: When people try to learn new material, they often cram, packing all their study into a single long session shortly before it is needed. Research in psychology suggests this is one of the least effective strategies. A more powerful approach relies on the spacing effect: the finding that information is remembered far better when study sessions are spread out over time rather than massed together. In a typical experiment, two groups learn the same set of facts for the same total number of minutes. One group studies everything at once; the other divides the study into shorter sessions separated by days. When both groups are tested weeks later, the spaced group consistently remembers more. Why this happens is still debated. One explanation holds that each time a memory is retrieved after a delay, the effort of recalling it strengthens the underlying trace, much as a muscle grows through repeated exercise. A gap between sessions forces the brain to work harder to reconstruct what was learned, and that extra effort pays off in more durable memory. The lesson for students is clear, if inconvenient: the comfortable feeling of fluency that comes from cramming is misleading, and genuine, lasting learning usually requires patience and repetition spread over time.

questions.q: What is the main point of the passage?
options: Cramming is the fastest and best way to memorize facts
Muscles and memories grow in exactly the same way
Psychology experiments are very difficult to design
Spreading study over time produces more lasting memory than cramming
questions.explanation: The passage introduces the spacing effect to argue that spaced study yields better long-term memory than cramming.

questions.q: In the experiment described, how do the two groups differ?
options: One group studies for more total minutes than the other
One group is tested immediately while the other is not
One group studies all at once while the other spreads study across days
One group learns much easier material than the other
questions.explanation: Both groups study 'for the same total number of minutes'; the only difference is that one studies 'everything at once' and the other in sessions 'separated by days.'

questions.q: It can be inferred that a student who feels very fluent right after cramming
options: may still forget much of the material later on
has definitely learned the material permanently
must have studied for fewer minutes than a spaced learner
will outperform a spaced learner on a delayed test
questions.explanation: The passage calls the fluency from cramming 'misleading,' implying that much of the material may be forgotten by the time of a later test.

questions.q: The word 'durable' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: recent
long-lasting
detailed
surprising
questions.explanation: 'More durable memory' is memory that endures, that is, is long-lasting.

questions.q: Why does the author compare memory to a muscle?
options: To prove that physical exercise directly improves test scores
To show that memory has no physical basis at all
To illustrate how repeated effortful recall can strengthen a memory
To argue that cramming builds mental strength
questions.explanation: The comparison illustrates the idea that effortful recall 'strengthens the underlying trace, much as a muscle grows through repeated exercise.'

title: The Printing Press and Literacy
passage: Before the middle of the fifteenth century, every book in Europe had to be copied by hand, usually by scribes working in monasteries. The process was so slow and costly that books were rare treasures, owned mainly by churches, universities, and the wealthy. The printing press with movable type, developed by Johannes Gutenberg around 1450, transformed this situation. By arranging reusable metal letters into pages that could be inked and pressed onto paper hundreds of times, a single workshop could produce in a week what once took a scribe years. The price of books fell steadily, and their numbers multiplied across the continent. This abundance had consequences that reached far beyond the book trade. As printed matter became affordable, more people had reason to learn to read, and literacy slowly spread beyond a narrow elite. Ideas could now travel faster and farther than before, carried in identical copies that were harder to suppress than a handful of manuscripts. Religious reformers, scientists, and political writers all seized on the new medium to reach wide audiences. Historians caution against crediting the press alone for the upheavals that followed, but few doubt that it accelerated the circulation of knowledge in ways that reshaped European society.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How the printing press made books cheaper and helped spread reading and ideas
Why monasteries stopped copying books entirely
The complete life story of Johannes Gutenberg
How raw metal is shaped into individual letters
questions.explanation: The passage traces how Gutenberg's press lowered the cost of books and thereby spread literacy and the circulation of ideas.

questions.q: According to the passage, how were books produced before Gutenberg's press?
options: They were printed rapidly in large workshops
They were imported from other continents
They were pressed from reusable movable type
They were copied by hand, often by scribes in monasteries
questions.explanation: The opening sentence states that 'every book in Europe had to be copied by hand, usually by scribes working in monasteries.'

questions.q: It can be inferred that printed ideas were 'harder to suppress' than manuscripts because
options: they were always written in a secret code
they existed in many identical copies spread widely
they were owned only by the very wealthy
they were locked away in monasteries
questions.explanation: The text contrasts 'identical copies' with 'a handful of manuscripts,' implying that widely distributed copies are harder to eliminate.

questions.q: The word 'abundance' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: shortage
expense
large supply
decoration
questions.explanation: 'This abundance' refers back to books whose 'numbers multiplied,' that is, a large supply.

questions.q: Why do the historians mentioned in the passage 'caution against crediting the press alone'?
options: Because the press had no real effect on society
Because books remained too expensive to matter
Because Gutenberg did not actually invent the press
Because other factors also contributed to the changes that followed
questions.explanation: The caution acknowledges that the press 'accelerated' change but was not the sole cause of the 'upheavals that followed.'

title: The Rise of Impressionism
passage: In the 1860s and 1870s, a group of painters working in France broke sharply with the conventions that had governed European art for centuries. Academic tradition prized smooth, invisible brushwork, carefully balanced compositions, and grand historical or mythological subjects finished in the studio. The painters who came to be called the Impressionists rejected much of this. They took their easels outdoors to capture landscapes, city streets, and ordinary people at leisure, and they worked quickly to record the fleeting effects of natural light. Rather than blending their colors into smooth surfaces, they applied paint in short, visible strokes that seemed to shimmer when seen from a distance. To many critics of the day the results looked unfinished, even careless; the very name "Impressionism," borrowed from a painting by Monet, was first used as a mockery. Yet what the critics dismissed was in fact a deliberate method. By placing small touches of contrasting color side by side, the painters let the viewer's eye do the mixing, producing a livelier sense of light than smooth blending could achieve. Rejected by the official exhibitions, the group organized shows of their own. Within a generation, the style they pioneered had reshaped Western painting and opened the way for even bolder experiments.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: The mythological subjects favored by academic painters
Why Monet decided to stop painting outdoors
How the Impressionists broke with tradition and changed Western painting
The rules for entering the official French exhibitions
questions.explanation: The passage describes how the Impressionists rejected academic conventions and, within a generation, reshaped Western painting.

questions.q: According to the passage, how did Impressionist brushwork differ from academic painting?
options: It used only a single color throughout
It used short, visible strokes rather than smooth blending
It was always completed inside a studio
It deliberately avoided the effects of natural light
questions.explanation: The Impressionists 'applied paint in short, visible strokes' instead of 'blending their colors into smooth surfaces.'

questions.q: It can be inferred that the name 'Impressionism' was originally intended to be
options: a precise scientific term
a sincere compliment
the official title of an exhibition
an insult
questions.explanation: The text says the name 'was first used as a mockery,' meaning it began as an insult.

questions.q: The word 'fleeting' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: quickly passing
brightly colored
carefully planned
widely admired
questions.explanation: The 'fleeting effects of natural light' are effects that pass quickly, which is why the painters 'worked quickly' to record them.

questions.q: According to the passage, why did the Impressionists place small touches of contrasting color side by side?
options: To make their paintings look unfinished on purpose
To let the viewer's eye blend them into a livelier sense of light
To use up less paint on each canvas
To imitate the academic tradition more closely
questions.explanation: The technique 'let the viewer's eye do the mixing, producing a livelier sense of light than smooth blending could achieve.'

title: The Principle of Comparative Advantage
passage: One of the most influential ideas in economics is the principle of comparative advantage, first set out clearly by David Ricardo in the early nineteenth century. It explains why two parties can both gain from trade even when one of them is better at producing everything. Suppose one country can make both cloth and wine more efficiently than another. It might seem that this country has nothing to gain by trading with its less efficient neighbor. Ricardo showed that this intuition is mistaken. What matters is not absolute skill but relative cost, what each producer gives up to make one good rather than another. If the more efficient country is only slightly better at wine but far better at cloth, it sacrifices less by concentrating on cloth and letting the other country supply the wine. By each specializing in the good it produces at the lower relative cost and then trading, both end up with more of both goods than they could have made alone. The principle does not claim that trade benefits every individual equally; workers in a declining industry may lose even as the nation as a whole gains. But it remains a powerful argument for why specialization and exchange can enlarge the total wealth available to all.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: One country is always better than another at producing every good
Comparative advantage explains why trade can benefit both parties even when one is more efficient
David Ricardo lived and worked in the nineteenth century
Wine is generally more valuable than cloth
questions.explanation: The passage uses Ricardo's principle to explain how both parties can gain from trade even when one produces everything more efficiently.

questions.q: According to the passage, what does comparative advantage depend on?
options: Relative cost, or what a producer gives up to make one good instead of another
The total population of each country
Which country discovered the principle first
The absolute skill of each producer considered alone
questions.explanation: The text says 'what matters is not absolute skill but relative cost, what each producer gives up to make one good rather than another.'

questions.q: It can be inferred that, according to Ricardo, a country that is better at producing everything
options: should never trade with anyone
will always lose from any trade
can still gain by specializing in what it produces at the lowest relative cost
must produce both goods entirely by itself
questions.explanation: Ricardo shows the 'intuition' that such a country gains nothing is 'mistaken'; it still benefits by specializing where its relative cost is lowest and trading.

questions.q: The word 'intuition' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: an official law
a formal trade agreement
a mathematical proof
an instinctive belief
questions.explanation: The 'intuition' that a superior producer gains nothing from trade is an instinctive, unexamined belief that Ricardo shows to be wrong.

questions.q: Why does the author mention 'workers in a declining industry'?
options: To acknowledge that trade does not benefit every individual equally
To prove that trade harms every nation involved
To argue that specialization should always be avoided
To show that Ricardo's principle is completely wrong
questions.explanation: The example qualifies the principle, noting that the nation may gain overall even though some individuals lose.

title: How Limestone Caves Form
passage: Most of the world's large caves are carved out of limestone, a rock made largely of calcium carbonate. Limestone has a crucial property: although it resists plain water, it slowly dissolves in water that is mildly acidic. Rainwater becomes weakly acidic as it falls through the air and seeps through soil, where it absorbs carbon dioxide. As this acidic water trickles into cracks in a limestone bed, it dissolves the rock along the way, widening the cracks over thousands of years into passages and, eventually, great underground chambers. The process does not stop once a cave is hollow. Water continuing to drip through the ceiling carries dissolved minerals with it. When a drop hangs from the roof and loses some of its carbon dioxide to the cave air, a trace of calcium carbonate is deposited. Drop after drop, over centuries, these deposits build the icicle-like formations that hang from cave ceilings, while matching mounds rise from the floor where the drops land. Because such formations grow at a slow but fairly steady rate, geologists can read them almost like tree rings, using bands within them to reconstruct patterns of ancient rainfall and climate long after the conditions that produced them have vanished.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: Why limestone is a popular building material
How the rings of trees record past climate
The dangers of exploring underground chambers
How limestone caves and their formations are created by acidic water
questions.explanation: The passage explains how mildly acidic water dissolves limestone to form caves and later deposits the minerals that build cave formations.

questions.q: According to the passage, how does rainwater become acidic?
options: By flowing over the surface of other caves
By freezing and thawing repeatedly
By absorbing carbon dioxide from the air and the soil
By dissolving calcium carbonate on the surface
questions.explanation: Rainwater 'becomes weakly acidic as it falls through the air and seeps through soil, where it absorbs carbon dioxide.'

questions.q: It can be inferred that a large cave chamber forms
options: during a single heavy rainstorm
very gradually, over thousands of years
only in places that contain no limestone
faster than a single mineral formation grows
questions.explanation: The text says acidic water widens cracks 'over thousands of years' into passages and eventually great chambers, implying very gradual formation.

questions.q: The word 'trace' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: a tiny amount
a sharp edge
a loud sound
a wide crack
questions.explanation: 'A trace of calcium carbonate is deposited' with each drop means only a tiny amount is left behind, which is why formations take centuries to build.

questions.q: Why does the author compare cave formations to tree rings?
options: Because both are actually made of wood
Because both grow only underground
Because both contain bands that record information about the past
Because both dissolve easily in acidic water
questions.explanation: Geologists 'read them almost like tree rings, using bands within them to reconstruct patterns of ancient rainfall and climate.'

title: The Iceman of the Alps
passage: In 1991, hikers in the Alps on the border between Austria and Italy came upon a human body emerging from a melting glacier. At first the authorities assumed it was a recent casualty of the mountains. Careful study soon revealed something far more remarkable: the man had died more than five thousand years earlier and had been preserved almost intact by the ice. Nicknamed the Iceman after the valley where he was found, he has become one of the most closely examined humans in the archaeological record. Because the freezing was so rapid and complete, not only his bones but his skin, internal organs, clothing, and equipment survived. Researchers have recovered his fur cloak, his shoes stuffed with grass for warmth, a copper axe, and a partly finished bow. The contents of his stomach revealed his final meals, and the pollen mixed in showed that he died in spring. Perhaps most striking, an arrowhead lodged in his shoulder indicates that he did not simply succumb to the cold but was attacked. Each of these details offers a rare, direct glimpse of daily life in the Copper Age, not through the ruins and refuse that archaeologists usually rely on, but through a single, extraordinarily well-preserved individual.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: A remarkably preserved ancient body that offers a direct glimpse of Copper Age life
How glaciers move slowly down mountain valleys
A border dispute between Austria and Italy
The modern manufacture of copper axes
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on the well-preserved Iceman and the direct evidence he provides about life in the Copper Age.

questions.q: According to the passage, how do researchers know that the Iceman died in spring?
options: From the recent melting of the glacier
From pollen mixed in with his stomach contents
From the copper axe that he carried
From the arrowhead lodged in his shoulder
questions.explanation: The text states that 'the pollen mixed in showed that he died in spring.'

questions.q: It can be inferred that the Iceman's body survived so completely because
options: he was carefully buried by other people
he lived only a few centuries ago
he was discovered by trained archaeologists
he was frozen quickly and remained encased in ice
questions.explanation: The passage says 'the freezing was so rapid and complete' that even soft tissues survived, so the ice is what preserved him.

questions.q: The word 'intact' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: frozen solid
recently dead
undamaged and whole
buried very deep
questions.explanation: Preserved 'almost intact' means preserved almost undamaged and whole, which the survival of skin and organs confirms.

questions.q: Why does the author mention the arrowhead in the Iceman's shoulder?
options: To show that he was a skilled hunter
To indicate that he was attacked rather than simply dying of cold
To explain how the glacier itself was formed
To prove that he had always lived in the Alps
questions.explanation: The arrowhead 'indicates that he did not simply succumb to the cold but was attacked.'

title: How Catalysts Work
passage: Many chemical reactions that are possible in principle proceed far too slowly to be useful. Before molecules can rearrange into new products, they must first pass through a high-energy, unstable arrangement, and the energy needed to reach this state acts as a barrier. A catalyst is a substance that lowers this barrier, allowing a reaction to proceed much faster without being consumed itself. Because the catalyst emerges from the reaction unchanged, a small amount can process an enormous quantity of material, cycling through the reaction over and over. Catalysts are central to both living cells and modern industry. In the body, protein molecules called enzymes act as highly specific catalysts, each speeding up a particular reaction that would otherwise be far too sluggish to sustain life. In factories, catalysts make it economical to produce fertilizers, fuels, and plastics on a vast scale, often by allowing reactions to run at lower temperatures and pressures than would otherwise be required, saving energy. It is important to note what a catalyst cannot do. It cannot force a reaction that is not already possible, and it does not change how much product ultimately forms; it only changes how quickly the system gets there. By speeding the path without altering the destination, catalysts quietly shape much of the material world.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Enzymes are the only catalysts that actually exist
Most chemical reactions are impossible without added heat
Catalysts speed up reactions without being used up, and are vital to life and industry
Fertilizers are made mostly of protein
questions.explanation: The passage defines a catalyst as something that speeds reactions without being consumed and shows its importance in both cells and industry.

questions.q: According to the passage, what is a catalyst able to do?
options: Force an otherwise impossible reaction to occur
Increase the total amount of product that forms
Become permanently consumed during the reaction
Lower the energy barrier so a reaction proceeds faster
questions.explanation: A catalyst 'lowers this barrier, allowing a reaction to proceed much faster without being consumed itself.'

questions.q: It can be inferred that a single catalyst molecule can process a large amount of material because
options: it is not consumed and can be used over and over
it changes into a new product each time
it raises the energy barrier of the reaction
it works only at very high temperatures
questions.explanation: Since 'the catalyst emerges from the reaction unchanged,' it can keep 'cycling through the reaction over and over.'

questions.q: The word 'sluggish' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: dangerous
slow
expensive
highly specific
questions.explanation: A reaction 'too sluggish to sustain life' is one that proceeds too slowly, which is why enzymes are needed to speed it up.

questions.q: Why does the author explain what a catalyst 'cannot do'?
options: To argue that catalysts are basically useless
To describe exactly how enzymes are manufactured
To prove that catalysts are consumed in reactions
To clarify the limits of a catalyst, which changes speed but not possibility or final amount
questions.explanation: The passage stresses that a catalyst 'cannot force a reaction that is not already possible' and 'does not change how much product ultimately forms,' defining its limits.

title: Partnership in a Coral Reef
passage: A coral reef looks like solid rock, but it is built by vast colonies of tiny animals called coral polyps. Each polyp is a soft, tube-shaped creature that draws calcium carbonate from seawater to construct a hard skeleton around itself. Generation after generation, these skeletons accumulate, forming the massive structures that shelter roughly a quarter of all marine species. Yet the polyps could not build reefs on their own. Living inside their tissues are microscopic algae called zooxanthellae. Through photosynthesis, the algae capture sunlight and produce sugars, most of which they pass to their coral hosts; in return, the coral supplies the algae with shelter and with the nutrients they need. This partnership is so productive that reefs flourish even in the clear tropical waters that are otherwise poor in nutrients. The relationship, however, is delicate. When the water grows too warm, the algae's chemistry goes awry and they begin to harm their hosts, so the coral expels them. Stripped of the algae that feed and color it, the coral turns a ghostly white, an event known as bleaching, and, if the heat persists, it starves. As ocean temperatures rise, mass bleaching has become one of the gravest threats facing these extraordinarily rich ecosystems.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: Why calcium carbonate dissolves in warm seawater
How corals and algae depend on each other to build reefs, and why that bond is fragile
The total number of fish species in the ocean
How photosynthesis works in land plants
questions.explanation: The passage describes the mutually beneficial coral-algae partnership that builds reefs and explains how warming can break it.

questions.q: According to the passage, what do the zooxanthellae provide to the coral?
options: A hard external skeleton
Protection from larger predators
Sugars produced through photosynthesis
Calcium carbonate drawn from seawater
questions.explanation: The algae 'produce sugars, most of which they pass to their coral hosts.'

questions.q: It can be inferred that coral bleaching is most likely to occur when
options: the surrounding water is unusually clear
nutrients in the water are especially plentiful
the coral builds its skeleton too quickly
ocean temperatures rise too high
questions.explanation: The passage links bleaching to warming: 'When the water grows too warm... the coral expels' the algae, turning white.

questions.q: The word 'expels' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: drives out
feeds
shelters
colors
questions.explanation: The coral 'expels' the harmful algae, that is, drives them out, leaving it 'stripped of the algae.'

questions.q: Why does the author describe the coral-algae relationship as 'delicate'?
options: Because coral skeletons are easily broken apart by waves
Because the algae cannot photosynthesize in tropical water
Because a rise in temperature can break the partnership and lead to bleaching
Because the polyps are far too small to see
questions.explanation: The word 'delicate' is immediately supported by the example of warming water causing the coral to expel its algae and bleach.

title: The Nature of Comets
passage: Comets are among the most spectacular visitors to the night sky, yet for most of their existence they are dark, frozen, and invisible. A comet is essentially a small body of ice, dust, and rocky grains, often described as a "dirty snowball," left over from the formation of the solar system. Most spend billions of years in the cold outer reaches beyond the planets, where they remain frozen and inert. Occasionally the gravity of a passing star or planet nudges one onto a path that carries it in toward the Sun. As the comet approaches, solar heat causes its ices to vaporize, releasing gas and dust that form a glowing cloud around the solid core. Radiation and the stream of particles flowing out from the Sun push this material away, producing the long, luminous tail for which comets are famous. Because the tail is blown outward by the Sun, it always points away from the Sun, not backward along the comet's path as one might expect. Once the comet swings around and heads back out, it cools, its tail fades, and it returns to darkness. Some comets follow orbits that bring them back on a regular schedule, so that a single comet may be seen again and again across the centuries.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: Why stars frequently pass close to the solar system
How dust forms on the surface of the outer planets
The exact return schedule of one famous comet
What comets are made of and why they grow bright tails near the Sun
questions.explanation: The passage explains the composition of comets and the process by which solar heat produces their glowing tails.

questions.q: According to the passage, what causes a comet's tail to appear?
options: Solar heat vaporizes its ices, and the released gas and dust are pushed outward
The comet collides with a nearby planet
The comet freezes solid as it nears the Sun
A passing star physically sets the comet on fire
questions.explanation: As the comet nears the Sun, 'solar heat causes its ices to vaporize,' and radiation and solar particles 'push this material away,' forming the tail.

questions.q: It can be inferred that a comet in the far outer reaches beyond the planets is
options: brightly glowing
trailing a long tail
dark and frozen
rapidly vaporizing
questions.explanation: The passage says comets there 'remain frozen and inert' and are 'dark, frozen, and invisible' for most of their existence.

questions.q: The word 'inert' in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: extremely hot
inactive
brightly lit
moving very fast
questions.explanation: In the cold outer reaches the comet is 'frozen and inert,' that is, inactive, with no tail or vaporization occurring.

questions.q: According to the passage, in which direction does a comet's tail point?
options: Away from the Sun
Backward along the comet's path
Toward the nearest planet
Toward the center of the galaxy
questions.explanation: Because the tail 'is blown outward by the Sun, it always points away from the Sun, not backward along the comet's path.'
